evidence.detail: 15 baseline and 15 Lean Protocol runs across the promotion suite.
evidence.label: Paired validation

evidence.detail: Both cohorts passed every verification gate in the 30-trial validation.
evidence.label: Verified reliability
evidence.limitation: This result applies to the recorded benchmark suite, configuration, and environment—not every model or coding task.

evidence.detail: Average latency improvement for Lean versus baseline across the paired validation.
evidence.label: Average speedup

evidence.detail: Average execution-time reduction across three fixed-environment tasks in an earlier, smaller spike.
evidence.label: Separate mini-spike
evidence.limitation: A three-task exploratory result reported separately from the 30-trial promotion validation.

buildRecord.detail: Baseline and candidate configurations execute the same versioned tasks in isolated workspaces.
buildRecord.label: 01 / CONTROL
buildRecord.title: Reproducible task suite

buildRecord.detail: Latency, token use, parser state, changed files, assertions, and failure categories are captured.
buildRecord.label: 02 / OBSERVE
buildRecord.title: Per-run telemetry

buildRecord.detail: Candidates must retain reliability, pass replay gates, and clear safety checks before becoming defaults.
buildRecord.label: 03 / DECIDE
buildRecord.title: Promotion protocol

buildRecord.detail: A compact command surface runs doctor checks, demos, benchmarks, and result inspection.
buildRecord.label: 04 / OPERATE
buildRecord.title: CLI and diagnostics

body: The evaluation harness, Lean promotion record, diagnostics, benchmark runners, and CLI are working. Research continues around model diversity, decomposition, autoresearch, and identifying configurations that generalize beyond a single suite.
currentStatus.title: Active research system with a usable operator surface.

decisions.consequence: Configurations can be compared on the result that matters: whether the task was completed safely and correctly.
decisions.decision: Score changed files and executable checks instead of grading the model’s explanation.
decisions.title: Verification outranks eloquence

decisions.consequence: The promoted configuration improved average latency without reducing the recorded pass rate.
decisions.decision: The Lean Protocol skips expensive planning behavior for task classes where verification proves it unnecessary.
decisions.title: Remove reasoning when evidence permits

decisions.consequence: A model response is not called successful unless it can survive the path from text to verified change.
decisions.decision: Output contracts, hidden state, fallbacks, and telemetry became first-class harness concerns.
decisions.title: Treat parser failures as system failures

failures.observed: An early decomposition strategy returned output the harness could not apply, producing zero-second parser failures.
failures.response: Standardized stdout, isolated hidden state, added metadata fallbacks, and captured per-pass telemetry before reevaluation.
failures.title: Decomposition produced parser-contract failures

failures.observed: The validation watchlist recorded a 37.6% latency regression for task_005 even though the task passed.
failures.response: Kept the result visible as a monitoring item rather than averaging it away.
failures.title: One network task regressed under Lean

failures.observed: Current evidence is tied to the benchmark suite and tested configurations.
failures.response: Expand model and task diversity before claiming general performance improvements.
failures.title: Broader local-model claims remain unproven

body: I designed and built the orchestration, task fixtures, response parsing, benchmark runners, telemetry, promotion gates, safety checks, and command-line interface.

The system runs third-party local model runtimes and foundation models. I am not claiming authorship of those models; the work is in making their behavior measurable, configurable, and operationally useful.
ownership.title: The harness is the product; the models are replaceable inputs.

body: Local coding models are attractive because they are private, inexpensive, and available without a network dependency. The hard part is not getting one impressive answer; it is producing useful changes repeatedly under real file, format, scope, and verification constraints.

Mighty Mouse turns prompt and harness decisions into controlled experiments. The same tasks run through competing configurations, and promotion depends on verified outcomes rather than subjective output quality.
problem.title: Small models can be capable and still fail the workflow.

systemMap.detail: A scoped coding problem with expected files and assertions.
systemMap.label: Task fixture

systemMap.detail: Executes the candidate and converts output into a deterministic change.
systemMap.label: Runner + parser

systemMap.detail: Tests, schema, scope, and mandatory-format checks decide success.
systemMap.label: Verification gate

systemMap.detail: Latency, tokens, pass rate, and regressions become an auditable decision.
systemMap.label: Promotion record

systemMap.detail: Parser errors, unsafe scope, or failed tests stop promotion.
systemMap.label: Abort / quarantine

projects.ownership: Original evaluation harness, benchmark workflow, protocol experiments, verification gates, and CLI. Model runtimes and foundation models are third-party systems.
projects.shortOutcome: An evaluation-driven harness for finding faster, more reliable ways to run small local coding models.
projects.title: Mighty Mouse

evidence.detail: The system is the working interface for active priorities, projects, reference material, and repeatable workflows.
evidence.label: Operational use
evidence.limitation: Personal records remain private; this case study uses a sanitized architecture and no private messages.

evidence.detail: Strategy, plans, weekly execution, active pipelines, offers, and workflows each have an explicit canonical home.
evidence.label: Routing model

evidence.detail: Update the existing owner, then weekly context, then inbox, and only create a file when no canonical home exists.
evidence.label: Write safety

buildRecord.detail: Strategy, execution, pipeline, offers, workflows, and inbox each have defined responsibility.
buildRecord.label: 01 / MAP
buildRecord.title: Canonical vault architecture

buildRecord.detail: Safe reads, write order, escalation points, permissions, and anti-sprawl rules are documented.
buildRecord.label: 02 / GOVERN
buildRecord.title: Agent authority contract

buildRecord.detail: Daily use is separated into focused contexts while sharing the same controlled operating layer.
buildRecord.label: 03 / INTERFACE
buildRecord.title: Telegram topic lanes

buildRecord.detail: Reusable tools connect conversations to real workflows instead of stopping at advice.
buildRecord.label: 04 / EXTEND
buildRecord.title: Skills and automations

body: LifeOps is not a public SaaS product. It is an operating system under continuous use, with new skills and workflow connections added when a repeated real-world need earns them.
currentStatus.title: Private, active, and used daily.

decisions.consequence: Responses stay grounded while reducing irrelevant context and accidental disclosure.
decisions.decision: The agent starts with indexes, the current weekly board, and targeted files instead of loading the vault.
decisions.title: Pointers before bulk context

decisions.consequence: The system resists the note sprawl that makes many second brains harder to trust over time.
decisions.decision: The write router updates the file that already owns a fact before creating anything new.
decisions.title: Existing truth before new documents

decisions.consequence: The system remains inspectable and useful outside any single agent session.
decisions.decision: Durable decisions are routed into explicit files with authority boundaries.
decisions.title: Conversation is an interface, not the database

failures.observed: Unconstrained capture duplicated facts and weakened the source-of-truth model.
failures.response: Added an explicit routing order and anti-sprawl rule that makes new files the last option.
failures.title: Agents tend to create a new note for every thought

failures.observed: The strongest evidence contains private people, plans, financial details, and conversations.
failures.response: Show architecture, redacted field shapes, and synthetic examples—not screenshots of the live vault.
failures.title: Personal context cannot become portfolio media

failures.observed: Personality, skills, and routing rules sit on top of an evolving upstream runtime.
failures.response: Keep custom contracts in inspectable files and test critical lanes after upgrades.
failures.title: Framework upgrades can disturb custom behavior

body: Hermes/OpenClaw supplies the general agent runtime. I built the working system around it: Telegram topic lanes, personality and response conventions, vault maps, memory boundaries, project skills, action permissions, and automations such as audio delivery and market execution.
The interesting engineering is the governance layer—deciding what the agent may read, where it may write, when it must ask, and which file remains authoritative after a conversation ends.
ownership.title: An upstream agent framework, heavily shaped into a personal operating layer.

body: Projects, people, ideas, client work, audio operations, and career decisions arrive through different tools and at different speeds. A conventional notes vault stores information, but it still depends on consistent manual filing and retrieval.
LifeOps gives the agent a controlled way to read current context, route new facts, and update existing sources of truth from the conversational surface I already use every day.
problem.title: A second brain fails when capture creates more maintenance.

systemMap.detail: Focused conversations separate projects and operational contexts.
systemMap.label: Telegram lanes

systemMap.detail: Targeted project records, workflows, and tool state are read only when relevant.
systemMap.label: Files + services

systemMap.detail: The weekly execution layer holds live priorities and next actions.
systemMap.label: Current board

systemMap.detail: Read/write lanes and permissions determine what the agent may change.
systemMap.label: Authority contract

systemMap.detail: Existing owner first; weekly board second; inbox if uncertain; new file last.
systemMap.label: Canonical router

systemMap.detail: A fact, task, decision, or workflow lands in its maintained home.
systemMap.label: Durable update

systemMap.detail: Structural edits, conflicts, and sensitive actions return to human review.
systemMap.label: Escalation

projects.ownership: I designed the LifeOps information architecture, authority boundaries, Telegram topic lanes, personality layer, skills, and automations. Hermes/OpenClaw is an upstream agent framework that I configure and extend rather than claim as original work.
projects.role: System designer, integrator, and daily operator
projects.shortOutcome: A private operating system that turns daily conversations into routed, durable context without surrendering human control.
projects.title: LifeOps + Hermes

body: The public directory, map-based discovery experience, studio pages, SEO controls, and production audit workflow are live. The active work is improving content quality, directory coverage, and the path from discovery to a confident studio choice.
currentStatus.title: Shipped and operating in production.

decisions.consequence: The product is designed around making a decision, not merely generating indexable pages.
decisions.decision: Treat content depth and comparison value as quality gates rather than maximizing listing count.
decisions.title: Directory usefulness before directory size

decisions.consequence: Visitors can reason about both studio fit and the practical cost of getting there.
decisions.decision: Combine structured filters with map context instead of forcing either list-only or map-only browsing.
decisions.title: Geography is part of the interface

decisions.consequence: Search acquisition is maintained as a testable system rather than a one-time metadata pass.
decisions.decision: Canonicalization, parameter handling, sitemap inclusion, and thin-content checks are explicit production gates.
decisions.title: SEO rules are product rules

failures.observed: Thin or repetitive directory pages risked weak visitor utility and weak search performance.
failures.response: Introduced content-depth checks, public-route probes, and an audit that flags thin indexable pages.
failures.title: More pages did not automatically create more value

failures.observed: Tracking and query variants can create duplicate or low-value route combinations.
failures.response: Canonicalize filter surfaces, redirect tracking parameters, and keep the sitemap restricted to intended public pages.
failures.title: Filter URLs can pollute the search index

failures.observed: Rates, rooms, equipment, and business status change independently of the application.
failures.response: Maintain a correction and update workflow; avoid presenting public-source data as permanently verified.
failures.title: Directory facts age

body: I designed and implemented the product, built its studio data and update workflow, created the map and discovery experience, established content and SEO standards, and operate the Vercel deployment.
Directory listings summarize public business information. A listing is not an endorsement, and accuracy depends on an ongoing maintenance loop.
ownership.title: A solo product spanning interface, data, and production operations.

body: Artists often compare studios across scattered social profiles, map listings, outdated websites, and word of mouth. Important differences—location, room type, equipment, services, and fit—are difficult to evaluate consistently.
The directory creates one browsable decision surface while giving individual studios durable, search-friendly pages.
problem.title: Finding a studio is a local-data problem disguised as a search box.

systemMap.detail: Public business sites, location data, services, rooms, and amenities.
systemMap.label: Studio sources

systemMap.detail: Normalized studio fields and editorial context form a maintained dataset.

systemMap.detail: Required fields, content depth, route integrity, and duplicate review.
systemMap.label: Quality checks

systemMap.detail: Search, filters, map context, recommendations, and detail pages.
systemMap.label: Discovery UI

systemMap.detail: Canonical metadata, sitemaps, index controls, and content audits.
systemMap.label: Search surface

systemMap.detail: Stale facts, closed studios, and thin records return to review.
systemMap.label: Correction loop

projects.shortOutcome: A production directory that turns a fragmented studio search into a location-aware, comparable decision.
